result:
I’d give every person an infallible, internal “sense of shared-consequences.”  
Not a rigid moral code or an imposed rule book, but a built-in intuition—strong as hunger, involuntary as pain—that makes you viscerally feel how your choices affect others.  
Steal someone’s idea and you feel their disappointment; cut down a forest and the pang of lost lungs for the planet registers within your own ribcage. Likewise, when you teach, heal, or plant, you savor the exhale of relief in others.

The mechanics don’t matter as much as the outcome: empathy stops being a fragile virtue that some cultivate and others ignore. What started as a pang becomes reflexive fairness. Markets adjust themselves to real externalities because corporate directors suddenly can’t bear the full weight of what dumping chemicals into rivers does downstream. War becomes obscenely less appetizing when every participant undergoes the terror they inflict. Climate negotiations flip from slow pageantry to urgent collaboration, because negotiators literally breathe the greenhouse gases they allow.

The beauty is that it also scales upward to the positive. Kindness turns infectious in the same brute-physical way: the plumber who fixes an elderly neighbor’s pipes at midnight walks home saturated in the warmth he created. The feedback loop tightens on generosity just as much as harm.

Would it erase all problems? No. Humans still compete for resources, grieve, and disagree. But the baselines shift: immediate cruelty becomes as unthinkable as biting off a finger, and systemic harm has to be rationalized against an innate bodily revolt.

One change: a neurological mirror polished so flawlessly that we can’t help but meet our own reflection in every face and ecosystem we touch.

prompt: If you could change the world in one way, what would it be?
